rationale_for_baseline_tier: With a clear kinetic trigger, habitual susceptibility and no central features on history, this is most likely benign kinetosis manageable with an antiemetic and sip rehydration — but acute vertigo/vomiting cannot be fully central-cleared via telehealth, and the patient (living alone) cannot keep fluids down, so prompt (24–48h, same-day if not improving) review with a low threshold is appropriate (T2). Central features carry a hard T5 safety-net; failed rehydration routes to ED (T4).

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden severe headache, weakness or numbness on one side, slurred speech, double vision, new deafness, or inability to walk/stand out of proportion to the nausea
escalation_edges.rationale: Possible posterior-circulation/cerebellar stroke — cannot be excluded remotely; time-critical.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a sudden severe headache, weakness or numbness on one side, slurred speech, double vision, new deafness, or you can't stand/walk beyond the dizziness — call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Still unable to keep any fluids down after the antiemetic, not passing urine, or marked persistent lightheadedness
escalation_edges.rationale: Dehydration from intractable vomiting may need parenteral treatment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you still can't keep fluids down after the anti-nausea medicine, stop passing urine, or feel very lightheaded — go to the Emergency Department.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with active fluid loss
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower threshold for ED if rehydration fails; ensure a plan to get help and monitor for central features.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Take the anti-nausea medicine, lie still with your head as steady as possible in a cool room, and rehydrate with a tiny sip of electrolyte drink every few minutes. It should settle over the next several hours, with a wobbly feeling for up to a day.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Antiemetic + sip rehydration + review within 24–48h; 000 for central features; ED if you can't rehydrate
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 immediately for any stroke-type features; ED if unable to keep fluids down.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Kinetosis after a rough boat trip; central (stroke) features screened negative on history but not examinable remotely; antiemetic + sip rehydration; dehydration and central safety-nets given.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Attributing to motion sickness with no central (stroke) screen or safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A cerebellar stroke is mislabelled as seasickness; the reperfusion window is missed.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: No dehydration safety-net in a patient living alone who can't keep fluids down.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling an ambulance for classic settling seasickness with a fully negative central screen — though a low threshold is reasonable given the limits of remote assessment.
